cataract: Distinguished by lens opacity causing a hazy or blurred fundus image, unlike the clear visualization in normal retina.
Cataract obscures retinal details, setting it apart from diabetic retinopathy and glaucoma, which show specific retinal changes.
This class is characterized by diffuse cloudiness and reduced red reflex, not seen in other retinal diseases.
Unlike diabetic retinopathy, cataract does not present with hemorrhages or exudates.
Cataract is easily separated from glaucoma by the absence of optic disc cupping or nerve fiber loss.
diabetic_retinopathy: Characterized by microaneurysms, hemorrhages, and exudates, diabetic retinopathy is distinct from other classes.
Diabetic retinopathy shows vascular abnormalities and retinal damage, unlike the structural changes of glaucoma or cataract.
This class is set apart by the presence of cotton wool spots and neovascularization, not seen in normal retina or glaucoma.
Unlike cataract, diabetic retinopathy does not cause lens opacity or diffuse haziness.
Diabetic retinopathy is easily distinguished from normal retina by the presence of multiple pathological lesions.
glaucoma: Distinguished by increased optic disc cupping and thinning of the neuroretinal rim, unlike the vascular changes of diabetic retinopathy.
Glaucoma shows structural changes in the optic nerve head, not seen in cataract or normal retina.
This class is characterized by loss of peripheral nerve fibers and pale optic disc, setting it apart from other diseases.
Unlike diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma does not present with hemorrhages or exudates.
Glaucoma is easily separated from cataract by the presence of optic nerve damage rather than lens opacity.
normal_retina: Shows clear visualization of the optic disc, macula, and vessels with no evidence of pathology, unlike all other classes.
Normal retina images lack hemorrhages, exudates, or optic disc changes seen in disease states.
This class is free of lens opacity, vascular abnormalities, or nerve fiber loss.
Normal retina serves as a baseline for comparison with all pathological retinal findings.
The absence of abnormal growths or structural changes distinguishes normal retina from all other classes.
